content:
Penalized regression grew from two parallel lines of work in the mid-20th century.

**Hoerl and Kennard (1970)** introduced **Ridge regression** to stabilize least squares when predictors are collinear—adding a small diagonal to XᵀX before inversion, later recognized as Tikhonov regularization from ill-posed inverse problems.

**Tibshirani (1996)** proposed the **Lasso** (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator), combining shrinkage with variable selection via an L1 penalty and sparking efficient algorithms such as coordinate descent.

The **Elastic Net** (Zou & Hastie, 2005) blended L1 and L2 to handle correlated groups while retaining sparsity.

Today Ridge and Lasso are default tools in statistical learning and production tabular ML—implemented in every major library and underpinning the broader L1/L2 regularization story across models beyond linear regression.

timeline.label: Ridge regression (1970)
timeline.description: Hoerl and Kennard stabilize OLS with an L2 penalty, addressing multicollinearity in econometrics and engineering.

timeline.label: Lasso (1996)
timeline.description: Tibshirani introduces L1 penalized regression for simultaneous shrinkage and feature selection.

timeline.label: Elastic Net (2005)
timeline.description: Zou and Hastie combine L1 and L2 to handle correlated predictors while encouraging sparsity.
